petitioner_background: The petitioner is a taekwondo athlete and instructor, apparently from Venezuela, seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in athletics under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). He served as Director of Promotions for a national taekwondo organization in Venezuela from 2010 to 2014, as president of a state-level chapter of that organization, and as founder and president of a martial arts academy he began in 2009. USCIS records indicate he resided in the United States during a relevant time period when he claimed continued involvement with the Venezuelan school.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of nationally or internationally recognized prizes for excellence in the field of athletics (specific awards not detailed in the decision text, but the Director accepted this criterion as met).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner satisfied the lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes criterion. No further analysis was provided because this criterion was not contested on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's participation as a judge of the work of others in the field (specific details not elaborated in the decision text, but accepted by the Director).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner satisfied the judging criterion. This criterion was not contested on appeal and was accepted as met.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted 31 articles and the transcript of a television interview. Most of the articles were in Spanish and accompanied only by summary translations. Of the articles in English or with full translations, two mentioned the petitioner: one from 1999 listed him among 23 individuals who won awards in an event, and another from 1999 identified him as a coach at one of 11 taekwondo schools in Venezuela. The petitioner also submitted a translated transcript of a television interview.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. First, because most articles were in Spanish with only summary translations rather than properly certified full English translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), the AAO could not meaningfully determine whether the translated material was accurate and supportive. Of the two English/translated articles mentioning the petitioner, neither was about him — they focused on events or organizations with which he was associated. The television interview transcript lacked original source material and relevant publication information, and review of the transcription showed it focused on taekwondo training in general and not the petitioner. Therefore, the evidence did not meet the plain language of the criterion requiring published material 'about the alien' relating to his work in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed leading or critical roles in three organizations: (1) Director of Promotions for a national taekwondo organization (held for five years, 2010-2014), supported by letters from the board of directors and the executive president describing responsibilities including heading assessment activities, determining evaluation procedures, developing evaluation plans, and updating training methods; (2) President of a state chapter of the organization, supported by letters describing duties such as leading affiliated schools in the state, organizing events, liaising with government officials, and overseeing three schools; (3) Founder and president of a martial arts academy (Escuela) he began in 2009, supported by a letter from the vice president describing responsibilities including handling legal formation of the corporation, selecting a location, hiring staff, supervising instructors, and training students. Additional evidence included a 1998 newspaper article about a tournament, a 2010 article about the national organization, a participation plaque, photographs of recent tournament trophies, and claims about special visitors attending events.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not establish leading or critical roles or distinguished reputations for any of the three organizations. For the national organization (Director of Promotions): the record did not demonstrate how the position fit into the hierarchy to show it was a leading role, and the letters described the role in general terms without supporting evidence of impact. The organization's distinguished reputation was not established — articles only referenced it in passing, two special visitors five years apart did not bestow distinguished reputation, the participation plaque did not identify the recipient as distinguished, and insufficient evidence showed the parent organization or its affiliate status conveyed esteem. For the state chapter: the petitioner relied solely on vague letters from the executive president and board of directors, failed to establish the organizational structure and hierarchy, and despite his title did not establish a leading role. A 1998 newspaper article about a prominent team predated his tenure by 11 years and was insufficient to show distinguished reputation; per Matter of Caron International, uncorroborated expert letters may be given less weight. For the martial arts academy: the letters briefly touched on his role without sufficient detail, per Matter of Y-B- there is greater need for corroborative evidence when testimonial evidence lacks specificity, and the record lacked corroborating evidence. The school's distinguished reputation was not established — individual attestations were unsupported, trophy photographs did not identify recipients, and USCIS records showed the petitioner resided in the U.S. during the tournament period without evidence of maintained active role.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only two of the ten regulatory criteria (awards and judging) rather than the required three. The petitioner failed to establish the published material criterion due to improperly translated articles and because the qualifying English-language materials were not about him, and failed to establish the leading or critical role criterion for all three organizations claimed because the evidence of his roles was vague and uncorroborated and the distinguished reputation of each organization was not demonstrated. Because the petitioner did not meet the initial evidence requirements, the AAO did not need to fully address a final merits determination, but noted that having reviewed the record in the aggregate, it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the level of expertise required for the classification sought.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: the petitioner submitted foreign-language (Spanish) articles with only summary translations rather than the full certified English translations required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which prevented the AAO from meaningfully evaluating most of the published material evidence. Additionally, USCIS records indicated the petitioner resided in the United States during the period when tournaments cited as evidence of the academy's success occurred, undermining his claim of active involvement with the Venezuelan school during the relevant time period.
